original_abstract:
The rapid expansion of the green energy sector and modern electronics has led to a dramatic increase in the global demand for rare earth elements (REEs). However, conventional industrial separation techniques, such as fractional crystallization and solvent extraction, are often energy-intensive, environmentally damaging, and operationally complex. Membrane-based separation processes have emerged as a sustainable, "green" alternative that integrates extraction and stripping into a single, efficient operation. While liquid membranes historically offered high performance, their industrial adoption has been severely limited by issues surrounding long-term stability and the leakage of organic extractants into the aqueous phase.

This review evaluates the recent shifts in membrane technology for REE recovery, with a primary focus on non-liquid strategies that address the durability challenges of their predecessors. We provide a comprehensive analysis of various innovative materials, including polymer inclusion membranes (PIMs), ion-imprinted membranes (IIMs), nanocomposites, and frameworks such as Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) and Covalent Organic Frameworks (COFs). The paper discusses the fundamental transport mechanisms—ranging from complexation-diffusion and facilitated permeation to Donnan and dielectric exclusion—that govern the selective isolation of REEs. Furthermore, we examine the technical performance of these systems, including extraction yields, selectivity between neighboring lanthanides, and membrane stability. Finally, the review addresses critical environmental and health considerations while outlining future research directions for the development of task-specific, high-performance separation materials designed to transition from the laboratory to industrial-scale application.
humanized_abstract:
The growing renewable energy sector and electronic industry has led to an ever increasing need for Rare Earth Elements (REEs). Conventional methods of separating REEs include fractional crystallization, and solvent extraction, which are highly energy consuming, harmful to the environment, and often difficult to manage. However, membrane-based separation process has emerged as a more sustainable or “green” approach. Integration of the extraction and stripping stages into a single stage membrane process enhances the efficiency of the separation process. Liquid membranes generally exhibited superior performance for metal extraction but their use in the industry has been severely restricted owing to shortcomings of membrane stability and the contamination of aqueous phase with organic extractants.

This review focuses on recent developments in membrane technologies for extraction of Rare Earth Elements (REEs) including a brief overview of recent developments of non-liquid membrane processes to address the operational lifetime challenges. Various new materials including polymer inclusion membranes (PIMs), ion-imprinted membranes (IIMs), nanocomposites, as well as more recently developed Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) and Covalent Organic Frameworks (COFs) are discussed. The possible transport mechanisms for separation of REEs using membrane processes, including complexation-diffusion, facilitated permeation, Donnan and dielectric exclusion are elaborated. Their performance including extraction efficiency, lanthanide separation selectivity and membrane stability are compared. Potential environmental and health impacts are also discussed. Future research efforts to realize new membrane materials at the industrial level that will be suitable for specific REE separations are also discussed.
